meta.project: ILM — Interactive Learning Material (V1, workflow v2)
meta.pipeline_step: Scenario generation (NOT in v2 diagram — see note)
meta.note: v0.3.0: realigned to updated rubrics.json — criterion 8.0 repurposed from 'Distinct (no duplicates)' to 'Example Requirement' (an example should appear only when the concept needs one); SCN-08 (flag: example present though not required) and SCN-08b (pass: example present because required) cover both polarities. WARNING: the rubric xlsx defines Example Scenarios but workflow v2 has no scenario node. Either add a 'Skill: scenarios' box to the per-concept loop or drop this sheet; keeping the eval set so either decision is covered.

source_spans.S1: An AI agent is a program that uses a language model to decide which actions to take. It runs in a loop: observe the current state, choose a tool, execute it, and feed the result back into the model. The loop ends when the model decides the task is complete.
source_spans.S2: Tool calling lets the model request that the host program run a specific function, such as a web search or a calculator, and return the result to the model.
source_spans.S3: A system prompt is a fixed instruction given to the model before the conversation starts. It sets the agent's role, rules, and output format.
source_spans.S4: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) fetches relevant passages from a document store and adds them to the prompt, so the model can answer using information it was never trained on. The model's weights are not changed.
source_spans.S5: A human-in-the-loop gate requires a person to approve the agent's output before it takes effect.

cases.edge_case: Scenario sounds like the concept but describes a different mechanism

input.candidate: A legal-tech startup uploads its case files so the model retrains on them overnight; the next day, the assistant answers questions about those cases.

cases.failure_mode: 'Answers questions about your documents' matches the RAG vibe, but retraining changes weights — the source explicitly says weights are not changed. This is the classic RAG/fine-tuning confusion.
cases.judge_instructions: Verify the mechanism in the scenario matches the concept's mechanism in the source span, not just the outcome.

cases.edge_case: Scenario mentions the concept by name but would fit any concept

input.candidate: A company adopts tool calling and finds their AI system becomes more capable and efficient, improving productivity across teams.

cases.failure_mode: Name-dropping the concept defeats naive relevance checks, but swap in 'RAG' or 'system prompts' and the scenario still reads fine — it shows nothing concept-specific in action.
cases.judge_instructions: Substitution test: replace the concept name with a sibling concept. If the scenario still makes sense, flag as generic.

cases.edge_case: Correct and relevant, but overloaded with incidental detail

input.candidate: A DevOps agent monitoring a Kubernetes cluster with 14 microservices observes p99 latency breaching 250ms on the checkout service, selects the kubectl-scale tool with replica delta +3 subject to the HPA ceiling, executes, then re-reads Prometheus metrics to decide whether a further scale event or a rollback of the 14:02 canary is warranted.

cases.failure_mode: Every detail is accurate and the loop structure is there — but a beginner drowns in Kubernetes specifics before seeing the loop. Pilot-phase human judgment per the rubric.
cases.judge_instructions: Reviewer: would a beginner extract the observe-act-repeat structure on first read? If the incidental detail obscures it, flag.

input.candidate: A bank's email-drafting agent writes responses to customer complaints. Each draft lands in a queue where a support lead approves or edits it. Only approved drafts are sent to customers.

cases.edge_case: Scenario is the explanation paraphrased with a thin narrative wrapper

input.explanation_text: A system prompt is a fixed instruction the model receives before the conversation starts. It sets the agent's role, rules, and output format.
input.candidate: At a startup, an engineer gives the model a fixed instruction before any conversation starts. This instruction sets the assistant's role, its rules, and its output format.

cases.failure_mode: Adding 'at a startup, an engineer' to the explanation is not a scenario. Compare candidate against the explanation_text — near-paraphrase means zero added understanding.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag if the scenario adds no concrete situation, stakes, or consequence beyond rephrasing the explanation. Provide the explanation text to the judge for direct comparison.

cases.edge_case: Playground key present but contains only a comment stub

input.concept: Tool calling (code concept: writing a tool function)

cases.failure_mode: Key-presence check passes; the playground contains no runnable starter code. Code grader must require at least one non-comment, non-blank line (or a successful parse with executable statements).
cases.judge_instructions: Code grader: strip comments and whitespace; flag if no executable content remains.

cases.edge_case: Scenario is in-domain (AI/software) but is not actually an instance of the concept

input.candidate: A data analyst asks a chatbot to summarize a quarterly report; the chatbot reads the document and returns a one-page summary.

cases.failure_mode: Same domain, plausible AI story — but it's a single-shot completion with no observe-act-repeat loop and no tool selection. In-domain non-instances are harder than the rubric's spreadsheet example.
cases.judge_instructions: Check the scenario exhibits the defining structure of the concept (here: a loop with tool choice and feedback), not merely the same industry or technology.

cases.edge_case: A self-evident concept gets an example anyway — example present though the concept does not require one

input.candidate: A system prompt is a fixed instruction given before the conversation starts; it sets the agent's role, rules, and output format. For instance, picture an engineer typing those role-and-rules instructions into a text box before chatting — that act is the system prompt.

cases.failure_mode: The concept is simple and self-explanatory from the explanation, so the rubric says no example is required. Tacking on an 'for instance' restatement adds an example the concept does not call for. The new criterion flags examples that appear even when the concept does not require one (it is no longer about duplicate scenarios).
cases.judge_instructions: Judge whether the concept actually requires a worked example to be understood. Pass only when an example is present because the concept needs it; flag when an example is supplied for a concept that is already clear without one.

cases.edge_case: A concept that genuinely needs an example to be understood gets one — example present because the concept requires it

input.candidate: A research agent is asked to find the cheapest flight. It observes the request, picks a flight-search tool, runs it, reads the results, then picks a price-comparison tool and runs that — repeating observe-choose-execute until it has an answer, at which point the loop ends.

cases.judge_instructions: The agent loop is a process concept that is hard to grasp from the definition alone, so a concrete example is warranted. Pass when an example is present because the concept needs it; this is the positive twin of SCN-08. A judge that flags a needed, on-concept example is over-strict.
